political.s: Per verified FEC data, Albertsons raised approximately $112K in PAC contributions with a left-leaning tilt (rep% split ~29% Republican / ~71% Democrat). OpenSecrets confirms the PAC raised $182,568 in the 2023–2024 cycle and gave $248,000 to federal candidates with roughly 57% to Democrats and 43% to Republicans. The company also spent $2.44 million on federal lobbying in 2024, largely focused on merger-related and food retail policy issues.

charity.s: Albertsons Companies Foundation's flagship Nourishing Neighbors program has raised over $297 million across a decade to fight hunger, with O Organics donating $7 million in 2024 alone. The Foundation holds a 3-star (82%) rating from Charity Navigator based on accountability and finance metrics. Cause areas also include disaster relief, veterans services, youth education, and disability-focused nonprofits, with over $65 million donated to disability-focused organizations since 2011.
charity.focus: Hunger relief, disability inclusion, disaster relief, veterans, youth education

environment.s: Albertsons reported a 21% reduction in its carbon footprint since 2019 and has set science-based targets to reduce operational emissions by 47% by 2030 and achieve net-zero by 2040 under its 'Recipe for Change' ESG framework. The company's entire private truck fleet is EPA SmartWay certified, and it diverted over 321 million pounds of food and trimmings from landfills in 2022 through composting and anaerobic digestion. Albertsons reports annually to CDP and uses third-party auditing for emissions data.

labor.s: Albertsons is one of the largest unionized grocery employers in the U.S., with a substantial portion of its workforce covered by collective bargaining agreements. However, the NLRB filed an amicus brief in 2024 criticizing Albertsons and Kroger for misrepresenting labor law during their failed $25 billion merger attempt, and investigators uncovered allegations the two companies colluded to suppress worker wages during a 2022 strike. Glassdoor ratings reflect mixed employee sentiment, and the failed merger raised significant labor concerns about store closures and bargaining leverage.

dei.s: Albertsons has an active and well-resourced DEI program, earning a perfect score of 100 on the 2024 Disability Equality Index for the second consecutive year and operating eight associate resource groups. The company runs an annual Supplier Diversity Program targeting minority-, women-, LGBTQ+-, veteran-, and disability-owned businesses. Glassdoor employees rate the company's DEI at 3.4/5, reflecting moderate internal satisfaction despite strong external commitments.

animals.s: Albertsons is a grocery and pharmacy retailer and does not manufacture cosmetics or personal care products that require animal testing. The company publishes an Animal Well-Being policy as part of its Recipe for Change ESG framework focused on responsible sourcing and humane treatment in its supply chain.

guns.s: Albertsons operates grocery and pharmacy stores and does not sell firearms or ammunition in its retail locations. The company's store banners — including Safeway, Vons, Jewel-Osco, and others — are food and drug retailers only.

privacy.s: Albertsons collects extensive customer data through loyalty programs, pharmacy records, apps, and online shopping, and shares data with third-party ad partners and affiliates. The company faced class action scrutiny over alleged use of the Meta pixel to share health-related purchase data with Facebook without customer consent, a potential violation of state privacy laws. Albertsons does provide a Privacy Rights Portal for opt-outs, data deletion, and CCPA compliance, and publishes annual CCPA metrics reports.

execPay.s: Former CEO Vivek Sankaran received total compensation of $15.24 million in fiscal 2024, while the median Albertsons worker earns a fraction of that; the SEC proxy for fiscal 2021 disclosed a CEO-to-median-worker pay ratio of 272:1, consistent with large grocery chains. Current CEO Susan Morris was paid $8.28 million in FY2024 (up 28%), and the compensation structure ties over 80% of executive pay to variable performance metrics. These ratios are well above the S&P 500 median and reflect the high disparity typical of large retail employers with many part-time hourly workers.
execPay.ratio: ~272:1 (FY2021 disclosed; FY2024 not yet publicly confirmed)
